content:
3 min read
Just now

--

A few months ago, I opened LinkedIn and immediately questioned whether I was on the right path.
Someone had landed a job at Google.
Another person had built an AI app over the weekend.
Someone else announced they had solved 500 coding problems.
Meanwhile, I was celebrating because I finally understood JavaScript’s map() function.
It didn’t feel like much.
The more I scrolled, the more I felt like I was falling behind.
For a moment, I even wondered if I should stop learning to code altogether.
The Comparison Trap
One of the hardest parts of learning programming isn’t the code.
It’s comparing your Chapter 1 to someone else’s Chapter 20.
Social media makes it easy to believe that everyone is moving faster than you.
What you don’t see are the months of confusion, failed interviews, abandoned projects, and late nights that came before those success posts.
You’re comparing your behind-the-scenes to someone else’s highlight reel.
That’s never a fair comparison.
Small Wins Still Count
When I first started coding, writing a simple “Hello, World!” program felt like an achievement.
Then I learned variables.
Then loops.
Then functions.
Each new concept felt difficult until it became familiar.
Looking back, I realized I had been making progress all along.
I just wasn’t giving myself credit because I was too busy looking at what everyone else was doing.
Progress Isn’t Always Visible
There were weeks when I felt like I wasn’t learning anything.
I’d spend hours debugging one small issue.
It was frustrating.
But when I revisited old projects, I noticed something surprising.
Code that once seemed impossible now looked simple.
That was proof that I had grown — even if I hadn’t noticed it day by day.
Learning often feels slow while it’s happening.
The progress becomes obvious only when you look back.
Everyone Learns at a Different Pace
Some people have computer science degrees.
Some have mentors.
Some have years of experience in related fields.
Some can study eight hours a day.
Others, like many beginners, squeeze in an hour after work or school.
Different starting points lead to different timelines.
Comparing them doesn’t make much sense.
What Helped Me Stay Motivated
Instead of comparing myself to other developers, I started comparing myself to the person I was a month ago.
Could I solve problems that once confused me?
Could I understand code that previously looked intimidating?
If the answer was yes, then I was moving forward.
That became my definition of progress.
My New Goal
I no longer aim to become the best developer in the room.
I aim to become a little better than I was yesterday.
Some days that means fixing a bug.
Some days it means reading documentation.
Some days it simply means not giving up.
Those small improvements add up over time.
Final Thoughts
If you’ve ever felt like everyone else is learning faster than you, you’re not alone.
Every developer starts as a beginner.
The people you admire today were once searching for answers to the same basic questions you’re asking now.
Keep learning.
Keep building.
And remember that the only comparison that truly matters is between who you are today and who you were yesterday.
Your journey doesn’t need to look like anyone else’s to be worth continuing.
